title: The farmer's son who tasted from the tin
blurb: A textbook 'lethal synthesis': an innocuous molecule that the cell's own enzymes convert into a tight-binding TCA poison.

patient.complaint: Found unresponsive in the locked equipment shed; mother says he 'was tasting things from a tin'.

opening: 4-year-old boy on a working farm. Found ~20 minutes after wandering into a locked equipment shed; he had a brief generalized tonic-clonic seizure en route. Initial pH 7.04 with a 32-point anion gap and lactate 12. The tin beside him reads 'Compound 1080 — sodium fluoroacetate — pest control'. Cardiac monitor now shows runs of ventricular ectopy. What do you want to ask EMS, examine, or order next?
history: Healthy boy, no prior ingestions. The farm uses Compound 1080 (sodium fluoroacetate) as a coyote bait. Empty tin found beside him.
exam: HR 178 irregular, BP 88/52, RR 30, T 36.6, SpO2 99% on 100% non-rebreather. Pale, diaphoretic, GCS 9. Cardiac monitor shows runs of ventricular ectopy. Abdomen soft. Pupils 4 mm reactive.

prompts.diagnosis: What is your diagnosis (be specific about the agent and the mechanism class)?
prompts.cause: What is the precise enzyme blocked after the 'lethal synthesis'?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why does this poisoning produce seizures, ventricular arrhythmia, AND hypocalcemia?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the TCA enzyme blocked by fluorocitrate.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — aconitase. The cell innocently converts fluoroacetate → fluoroacetyl-CoA → fluorocitrate, which then ties up the very next enzyme. Citrate piles up; TCA flux collapses.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. Fluoroacetate isn't the inhibitor — the body BUILDS the inhibitor (fluorocitrate). Which enzyme acts on citrate?
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: Aconitase (Citrate → Isocitrate). Fluorocitrate is a near-irreversible tight-binding inhibitor — the textbook 'lethal synthesis'.

mcqs.stem: Why is fluoroacetate called a 'lethal synthesis' poison?
choices: The molecule is harmless until the cell's own enzymes convert it into a tight-binding inhibitor (fluorocitrate)
It synthesizes a lethal antibody to TCA enzymes
It triggers de novo synthesis of pro-apoptotic factors
It is the product of an esterification done in vivo
mcqs.explanation: Coined by Sir Rudolph Peters in 1952. Fluoroacetate is itself benign; the cell's own machinery (acetyl-CoA synthetase, then citrate synthase) builds fluoroacetyl-CoA → fluorocitrate, which then ties up aconitase. The poison is made by the victim.

mcqs.stem: Why does the patient develop hypocalcemia and ventricular arrhythmia?
choices: Citrate chelates ionized calcium in plasma
mcqs.explanation: Citrate is a calcium chelator — the same reason blood-bank PRBCs (citrated) drop ionized calcium in massive transfusion. Massive citrate elevation lowers ionized Ca²⁺, prolonging QT and predisposing to ventricular arrhythmia, and lowers seizure threshold.

mcqs.stem: Why does serum citrate climb so high in this poisoning?
choices: Aconitase is blocked, but citrate synthase keeps producing citrate from acetyl-CoA + OAA
Citrate synthase is activated by fluorocitrate
The kidney reabsorbs citrate avidly under acidosis
mcqs.explanation: Citrate synthase is upstream of the block and keeps running. With aconitase locked, citrate can't proceed to isocitrate — it accumulates intracellularly and spills into plasma.
